aliases: ปีกไก่กะเพรา

thai: ปีกไก่กะเพรา

food_subgroup: Poultry — chicken wings, marinated and stir-fried

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product class; no specific Thai basil preparation subclass exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers chicken wings as a category; no Thai basil marinade-specific code exists. closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Thai basil chicken wings as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 5 chicken wings, approximately 300 g total, as typically served at a Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken wing, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 05064) adapted with Thai basil stir-fry seasoning profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base macros derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken wing (NDB 05064, roasted, meat and skin). Sodium and fat values adjusted upward to reflect Thai basil stir-fry preparation with fish sauce (~400 mg Na/100 g additional contribution), oyster sauce, and frying oil. Saturated fat estimated from USDA chicken wing skin data. Sugar estimated from oyster sauce and sugar seasoning. Dietary fibre estimated from basil and vegetable components. Values represent a best-estimate composite for a restaurant-prepared Thai basil chicken wing dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil chicken wings. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous stir-fried chicken dishes with minimal carbohydrate content from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with very low carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g), yielding a low GI category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 9.6) / 100 ≈ 3.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 9.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce (~620 mg Na per 100 g; ~1860 mg per 5-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Thai basil chicken wings due to the very high sodium load from fish sauce and oyster sauce; a single 5-piece serving may contribute over 80% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic load per serving (~3.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai basil chicken wings have a low glycemic load and minimal carbohydrate content, making them a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments; however, the high sodium content warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (4.6 g/100 g) and high sodium (~620 mg/100 g) from stir-fry seasonings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Thai basil chicken wings in moderation due to the combined burden of saturated fat from chicken skin and high sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce, both of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Chicken Wings (5 pieces) is a Thai-style dish consisting of chicken wings marinated and stir-fried with Thai holy basil (krapao), garlic, fresh chilli, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and a small amount of sugar. The dish is popular in Thai restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Sanook Kitchen. The chicken wings are typically cooked with the skin on, resulting in a crispy exterior with a savoury, aromatic, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The holy basil imparts a distinctive peppery, clove-like fragrance that differentiates this dish from sweet basil preparations. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~19.5 g/100 g) and moderate-to-high in fat (~16.8 g/100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g/100 g), making it a low-glycemic-load food. However, the sodium content is high (~620 mg/100 g) due to the use of fish sauce and oyster sauce, which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, fish, or shellfish allergies. In Chinese, the dish is known as 九层塔鸡翅膀, and in Thai as ปีกไก่กะเพรา. It is commonly served as a main dish or shared plate alongside steamed jasmine rice.
common_user_queries: Is Thai basil chicken wings healthy?
How many calories in Thai basil chicken wings?
Is basil chicken wing suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium in Thai basil chicken wings?
Is Thai basil chicken wing halal?
ปีกไก่กะเพรา nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Thai basil chicken wings exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Macros are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken wing data (NDB 05064) with seasoning adjustments for the Thai basil stir-fry preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for stir-fried chicken dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (cooked chicken wing with skin) is well-characterised in USDA data and the seasoning adjustments are conservative and documented.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g reflecting trace levels from chicken skin; this is not confirmed zero. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Sanook Kitchen - Funan) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment in Singapore; consumers requiring halal certification should verify independently. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu string '104D Basil Chicken Wing (5pcs) 九层塔鸡翅膀 (五' to the canonical English form 'Thai Basil Chicken Wings (5 pieces)'. The Chinese name 九层塔鸡翅膀 is preserved from the input record. Serving size of 300 g is estimated for 5 medium chicken wings including bone; edible portion is approximately 180–200 g. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (35 × 9.6 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 3.4.
